explanation: The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is typically written as globally (G), stating that at all times, if prop_0 is true then prop_1 is true.
The natural language 'If prop_0, prop_1' can be interpreted as 'whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time.' This is the standard implication: globally, prop_0 implies prop_1.
This statement says 'if prop_0, then prop_1.' In LTL, this is expressed by globally (G) asserting that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time.

The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' expresses an implication: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this is captured as: globally, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true. Therefore, we use the globally (G) operator and express the implication.

The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' is a simple implication: whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that moment. In LTL, this is written as globally (G) - for all times, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold.

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' represents an implication: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold (at that same time). This is written as an implication between prop_0 and prop_1, holding globally (at all times).
The input expresses a simple conjunction: both prop_0 and prop_1 should be true. In LTL, this is written as prop_0 & prop_1.
The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' is interpreted as 'whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold'. In LTL, this can be expressed as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1', which is G(prop_0 -> prop_1).

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' captures a conditional implication, meaning whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same moment. In LTL, this is classically expressed as globally (G): G (prop_0 -> prop_1).
The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' usually means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this is best captured as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1', i.e. G (prop_0 -> prop_1).
